content:
The **conditional independence assumption** did not arrive with a single named theorem—it emerged as the practical modeling move that made **Bayes' theorem usable for multi-feature classification**.

Early **spam filtering** work in the late 1990s and early 2000s (notably **Paul Graham**'s essays and filters, and contemporaneous Bayesian email classifiers) popularized multiplying per-word likelihoods under a class label. Practitioners needed to classify high-dimensional text with modest data; estimating full joint word distributions was impossible, but per-word counts per class were not.

Statistically, the factorization connects to **naive Bayes networks**—directed models where the class node is the sole parent of every feature node, encoding that features are independent given the class. **Pearl**'s work on graphical models (1980s) formalized this structure, though the "naive" label reflects the field's skepticism about the realism of the assumption.

**Multivariate** analysis had long used independence as a simplifying approximation when **Hotelling**, **Fisher**, and others developed multivariate normal theory; **Fisher's Iris data** (1936) later became a standard testbed where Gaussian NB applies the same idea to continuous features.

In modern ML, naive Bayes remains the **canonical fast baseline** for text and tabular problems—less because independence is true, and more because the product form **P(x|y) = ∏ P(xᵢ|y)** scales where joint models cannot.

timeline.label: Bayes & inverse probability (1763+)
timeline.description: Bayes' theorem provides P(y|x); practitioners still need a tractable model for P(x|y) when x has many components.

timeline.label: Graphical models (1980s)
timeline.description: Bayes nets with a single class parent formalize conditional independence among features given a hidden label.

timeline.label: Bayesian spam filters (late 1990s–2000s)
timeline.description: Product-of-word-likelihood filters demonstrate production-scale naive Bayes for email; per-token factors become standard engineering practice.

timeline.label: sklearn & ML baselines (2010s–present)
timeline.description: GaussianNB, MultinomialNB, and BernoulliNB in libraries encode the assumption explicitly; naive Bayes remains a first classifier taught and deployed for text and sensor fusion.
